speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Hello everyone. Welcome to a new episode of The Dissenter. I'm your host, as always, Ricardo Lops, and today I'm joined by a return guest, Dr. Daisy Dixon. She's a lecturer in philosophy at Cardiff University, and today we're going to talk about her book Depraved The Story of Dangerous Art. So Dr. Dixon, welcome back to the show. It's always a pleasure to talk with you.

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Ah, thank you, it's great to be back. Thanks for having me.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: No, I, I, I mean, it's awesome to have you back. I really loved the book, by the way, and so it's, I'm really excited to finally have you on the show again to talk about it. So, uh, but let me start with this question. I, I, I know that we could go down a big rabbit hole. Here because I know that people who work in aesthetics, there's a huge never ending discussion about what is art, what counts as art, and all of that, but I mean, in your book, do you use a specific definition of it? I mean, what do you mean by art

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: exactly? Yeah, so I kind of take a sneaky approach in the book. I kind of avoid trying to define art. And in part that's because art itself is a tricky kind of concept to get clear on. And I think because the function of the book wasn't to necessarily get clear on a definition. I kind of play with a few sort of popular definitions that it's like a human made object, perhaps intended to communicate some kind of meaning. Or indeed to like have certain aesthetic properties. I also skirt around the institutional, uh, definition of art where it's just any object or any event or thing, um, that has been sort of granted the status of art by a certain authority like an art world or certain experts. Um, BUT in the book, I end up just sort of going for it I know it when I see it. Uh, APPROACH, which basically means that I kind of do it by extension instead. So I include things like traditional art objects like uh painting and sculpture, literature, like, you know, novels and film, video art, but I also go into like more contemporary pieces like installations, performance, uh, but I also include video games as well. Um. And that's in part that has had a little bit of um not pushback, but sort of surprise from some commentators in the general public at least. And obviously philosophers of art have been interested in video games for a long time. There's a huge area of like philosophy looking at video games now. And I think that's in part because of its fictional worlds, most of the time. Um, AND the fact the viewer can kind of co-create those fictional worlds is an important thing. So yeah, quite a wide. Conception

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: so you've been getting pushback from the general public when it comes to labeling video games as art, is that it?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, I mean, not much pushback, just some surprise, I guess, and I think this has been an interesting thing, writing a book for the general public, um, taking kind of philosophical assumptions of what's done in academia. And philosophers of art have been looking at video games for a long time as a kind of art form. And I do see them as an art form, but I think there are still some people, uh, in the general public that would that would kind of put our video games into a different kind of category. Um, BUT I think they are an art form, even if they're particularly. Peculiar, especially in the fact that the viewer, the player is co-creating that fictional world at the same time.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Well, I mean, I guess that it could be argued that whenever people are interpreting a work of art, in at least to some extent they are sort of co-creating.

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, yeah. And that is a balance I play with, uh, in the book, the trying to marry that tension between the artists kind of act. I see I see artworks is kind of peculiar forms of art and communication, uh, often using, um, Aesthetics or a material in a way to communicate something sometimes across, um, dimensions, sometimes depending on what your religious beliefs are, also across, um, societies and across time as well. Um, SO even if we don't see them as like ordinary language, I see them as acts of communication. And so in that you Encounter similar issues with communication as to who is creating the meaning, and I try and go for a kind of, the artist is really important and their social identity, their placement in history is really important to, Getting out what an artwork means, but the viewer absolutely has agency. In reaching that meaning and as I go on later in the book to look at actually destroying that meaning as well.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Mhm, YEAH, we will certainly get back into that topic later in our conversation. I mean, because you're talking about ethics in your book, the ethics surrounding art, do you approach it through any specific ethical theory, ethical system, or not?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, so kind of like the art definition question, I don't settle on a particular moral theory. So I do glance at um a few normative. MORAL theories like consequentialism, like the ontological theories. I also dance over kind of meta ethical questions of is there such a thing as a moral fact? And if so, is it determined by, um, something natural that could be determined by sort of scientific investigation, empirical investigation, or is it a non-natural thing like, um, Exists in some kind of abstract realm that we detect via intuition, um, and reason. So, What I do do though is adopt a kind of objectivism. So I I kind of reject moral relativism. I also reject a nihilistic approach. I do think there is such a thing as right and wrong, good and bad. Um, I As I say, I don't settle on exactly what makes something good and bad. It could, for instance, be. You know, bad consequences that result in just pain, um, or it could be a violation of a moral law that we've reached either from some divine being or just via reason, um. But I do go for an objectivism, so I kind of say that. There are objective things that are wrong, because the book is looking at immorality in particular. There are certain things that are wrong and bad, and I say these aren't going to be up for discussion. So I give examples of that, um, like, Um, sexual violence, um, enslavement of people, and then kind of more abstract, uh, not abstract, but more structural systems, um, like racism, sexism, transphobia, ableism, ageism. And general social inequalities like the unfair distribution of wealth and things. I think these things are objectively bad, and they are bad, this is the other thing that's been slightly controversial for the public is, you know, Someone judging artworks based on a seeming moral system of today. But that's I've always found that very problematic, kind of relativistic position. Like I'm sure the millions of people who've been slaved in, you know, for instance, in the transatlantic slave trade, the millions of black women, men and children, I'm sure. They wouldn't have judged that that at the time that that was OK. So we need to be careful who the kind of moral judges are when we talk about moral relativism.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Mhm. Talking about that actually, when it comes to how we should be careful about who the moral judges are when it comes to moral relativism, or different kinds of moral perspectives that people might hold, uh, I wanted to ask you about the term you use in your, in your book, depravity, because I, I mean, at least in Portuguese culture where I live, but that with Imagine that also in the UK and other places we tend to hear terms like depraved or degenerate more from people who are on the right wing of the political spectrum, conservative people, religious people. Do you think that using such a term, with using such a term, there could be a risk that we would be serving? The interests of those kinds of people and for example, I'm just going to use an example. People could use the term depraved to refer to creations by gay artists, for example, someone might use that term to condemn the works of Freddie Mercury, let's say. So what, what do you think about that?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: I think that's a great point. And I think that is something that came out when I was writing the book, um, but also, um, beginning to, uh, learn. And so one author said to me that when you start touring a book, you actually learn what it's about, um, after you've written it. I do think that's kind of true. So I think that's a really good point about the connotations of the word depravity absolutely seem to be. And I think that is also sort of the case in the UK. It's a word often reached by and used by kind of right wing commentators, often in tandem with obscene, as you say, degenerate, smutty, um, and it's often used to, uh, denounce. Art by marginalized artists or indeed art that presents sex in a way that doesn't fit with kind of dominant whatever the particular religion, let's just say Christian, a kind of dominant Christian doctrine of what sex should or can look like. Um, SO I do, so I think when I was using the word depravity and in the book I say that I'm thinking about it in terms of something that's extremely immoral. Um, SO, you know, a white lie, telling you a white lie to make you feel good might be technically wrong, but we wouldn't really say it's depraved or like extremely immoral. Or maybe Emmanuel Kant would, but, you know, it's bad, it's bad insofar as it's a lie. It's it's not like the worst thing we could do. And then of course there's a spectrum and then we go all the way from. Um, ABUSE of another human being to genocide, right? And so when I use it, I mean extremely immoral. But what was interesting in writing the book is that I did notice that it was being used, um, by some right wing commentators to describe art that is conventionally, uh, deviant or deviates from kind of what we deem to be conventionally acceptable, depending on what society we live in. Um. And so in a way, the book has become a kind of attempt to reclaim that word, um, whilst there's this kind of philosophical, like this is what I mean when I use the word, it's also kind of become this thing of, Like one newspaper reported, did an interview with me and it said, Daisy Dixon's standing up for depravity. And I was like, OK, but in a way that's true. Because if, if when if what they, if what some people think is depraved, and it's just like queer art. Then yeah, like, so maybe it's an attempt to reclaim what that what depravity actually means as well.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Yeah, yeah, that's a very interesting approach. I've had conversations with feminist scholars on the show where they told me that, I mean, it's been something that feminists have also done in recent decades is to reclaim the B word, for example, right? I mean, I mean turning it into something that is not really. Uh, PEJORATIVE, but something that feminists themselves and women more generally can use, so I mean there's that kind of approach as well.

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, definitely. And I think, um, obviously the reclamation of slurs is a tricky thing because, um, different groups who are the target of that slur, different people in that group are going to disagree that for instance, uh, many black people do not want to reclaim the N word, even though some do. Um. You know, some specifically sexist slurs. I don't like any of them. So I don't like want to reclaim any of them. Um, BUT I think with depraved, there is something potentially quite powerful that we can do with that. So if you put an asterisk against it, maybe, like, yes, we should all be depraved and that, you know, this is an important thing to be to be transgressive is important. But doing that with an objective moral system in place, like to call queer art depraved. Is wrong insofar as you're saying it's extremely immoral, it's not, but if we want to use depraved with like an asterisk or something, then, yeah, let's all be depraved in that way. Yeah.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: So we're going to go through the several different ways you explore in the book as to how works of art can be moral, but do you think that artworks themselves can be depraved, to use your terminology, independently of whether the artist, him or herself, is depraved or independently of their intentions?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, so Whether or not an artwork itself is depraved is a tricky question because it can relate to immorality in ways that might not necessarily mean that it itself is depraved. So for instance, an artwork might cause or inspire someone to do something terrible, but it not necessarily be immoral in itself. It doesn't have those like moral properties as part of the actual work of art. Um, BUT there are cases when, An artwork itself is depraved insofar as um its relation to morality pertains to its actual properties as in regards to its meaning and what it's about and what it's doing. Um, SO that's in terms of its moral perspective. So it's not simply that it's depicting something immoral, um, it's when the artwork is inviting the viewer to adopt a problematic attitude, or we might say immoral attitude to what's being depicted. So, you know, for instance, an artwork that glamorizes sexual violence or champions, um, you know, enslavement. Um, Or celebrates a totalitarian regime, like a lot of Nazi propaganda, for instance, would do. Um, SO in that sense it's moral perspective is is part of its very identity as an artwork. So in that sense it can be depraved. However, As you rightly say, how that relates to the artist's intention is a tricky one. Most of the time. Or a lot of the time I'd say that the the reason the artwork has that perspective is in part because of the artist's intention. Um, Leni Riefenstahl's Triumph of the Will, which philosophers of art always use as an example, um, Thematically when just watching it without any kind of not like background knowledge, you can kind of see that it's it's a celebration or very positive looking film, um, about the Nazi regime and it's celebrating that. The fact that Leni Riefenstahl actually, um, believed in that is a way to kind of confirm it's immoral perspective because it's inviting us to do as viewers to do something wrong, um. But it's very, sometimes it can be the case that. An artist has a good intention. But the artwork's perspective kind of runs away from them. So this again, this is the problem of, yes, the artist is important, but they don't have complete authority over what their work not only can can do, but like indeed what it means as well, so. There are a few examples of this, um. One example would be Brett Bailey's exhibit B piece, um, from I think 2013. Um, Brett Bailey is a white South African man and in these, um, performances they were kind of like, uh, installations with performing actors, like living, um, kind of tableaus and. He was trying to expose the history of the human zoo and of um. The exploitative relations between Europe and Africa, particularly in the 19th century with the human zoos that were particularly prevalent in France and the UK. Um, AND he, he had good intention for it. Like that that's a morally laudable like thing to do. But because of the content of the work, because it involved actual black people, actors who had consented. But it involved them being chained up and put on display in this sort of recreation of the human zoo, and many people, not all, but many people said that the show was racist as a result, even though the art, you know, Brett Bailey intended the complete opposite, and there are other examples of this happening, so. Sometimes, uh, an art artworks more perspective might be distorted or indeed it it fails. Perhaps because of the way it's been curated. Maybe Brett Bailey's piece was wasn't adequately curated because people said it was re-performing the very power relations it was meant to critique. And that in part is because maybe Brett Bailey's identity is a white South African man. Mhm. Kind of put him in an impossible relation with his own work where um that power asymmetry that is historically significant. Was kind of unavoidable. So yeah, sometimes an artwork can have a more perspective that. Moves away from what the artist intended, which is unfortunate when that happens, because the show was closed down, it was canceled.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Yeah, I mean, you know, probably we're going to talk more about this later, but nowadays I'm very, I mean, I'm much more suspicious about more artists because of unfortunately over the years people uncovering the bad deeds of men, more and more artists out there. So for example, I used to be a very big fan of Neil Gaiman and Now I completely despise the man, and for example, now I know that in The Sandman, in one of the chapters, it depicts an episode where there's a man who basically is holding a woman hostage in in his apartment and he's exploiting her sexually to, I mean, get inspiration for his art and then Morpheus, I mean, basically. The Sandman saves the woman, and I mean now we know that Neil Gaiman was doing more or less the same with with a woman actually. So I mean sometimes I get more and more suspicious about if a particular author, particularly if he or she is very fixed on a particular depiction of a particular act. I mean nowadays I'm like, I mean, is it really just Art or is it that the person herself has those kinds of depraved thoughts and depraved desires, you know. Yeah,

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: exactly, and I think a lot of people are really struggling with everything that it seems continues to come out about Neil Gaiman and how that's affecting their engagement with his work, with his art, and I think in that case, the, It's almost as if depending on what the artist's intention was, er and what their motivation was in making that work, that can affect the very properties of that artwork, which would mean that even people who aren't aware, cos often these things as you say, come out over time. I think it's not the case sometimes that the artwork changes in meaning. Unfortunately, sometimes, again, if that intention and motivation is there, if Gaiman was deliberately sort of imagining that scene and giving it to the reader in a way that he was getting some kind of gratification from it or arousal from it, that unfortunately is part of the artwork's meaning and identity, even if the reader didn't know about it. So I think there's this interesting case of when artworks are these. Incredibly rich objects that we can continue to discover their properties, their meanings, their aesthetic properties. Perhaps those works are now ugly, as a result. Perhaps, you know, they, their artistic value has gone down. If you think the artistic and the moral realms interact in that way, if an artwork is immoral, it might make it worse as an artwork if you're a particular moralist in the debate, um. But it's almost as if I'd say those properties were always there, even if we couldn't detect them. But I do think as well with the moral artist problem. There are different ways that that moral character can come out, but it seems like in Neil Gaiman's case, I mean, I've only read one of his books, and I can't recall that it, you know, that it had anything egregiously, you know, relevant to his own crimes and his misdeeds. But your example is fantastic, er, is a great example of how an artist's immorality is kind of evidenced by the, Uh, artwork, and in that case it is obviously a problematic connection that would indeed affect how you interpret the, the story, whatever it is.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Uh, uh, WHAT do you think, I, I mean, and, and also, uh, along the same line of thought of us over time uncovering depraved acts by artists. I mean, sometimes I, I find myself thinking about this. Do you think that we as consumers of art should be held responsible for, I don't know, of course, some of these. Acts we can't just find them out for ourselves. I mean, it has to be the proper authorities doing that and then it coming out to the public. But do you think that we should perhaps be a little bit more responsible with searching as best we can, the life of the particular artists, the particular creators?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, that's a really good question. I think we need to be careful about being placing sort of those level of demands on consumers of our, especially in our society at the moment where we're all sleep deprived in in different ways depending on gender and race, things like that. There's like a sleep gap, there's a gender pay gap, there's like. And I feel like to to consume art in this day and age is already quite a privileged thing to do. And we all have so little time. So I'd be wary of kind of saying, yeah, that it was the responsibility would fall on on the viewer to like really detect the true or the kind of core properties that are in that work that they might not know if they just consumed it. And that's why I think. I wouldn't necessarily blame someone for just escaping in Neil's, for instance, Neil Gaiman's um stories. I wouldn't blame someone necessarily for doing that, but I do think what we do have a responsibility to to do is when we are told, or when we do others, as you say, of some other way find out, we do have a responsibility to. Think critically about our relationship. With that artwork, like for instance. When I was growing up, I didn't like go again, but that's just because he upset Van Gogh, who's like my all time favorite artist, um. That's like, OK, like that, you know, that's, whatever reason I, and I actually never really liked his paintings anyway, and it might be because I as a young girl, I was looking at them, I was like well, I'm looking at a young girl sort of naked on the bed and as a kid I didn't have the vocabulary to really articulate why that was a bit gross. Um, BUT maybe there was something in me that was a reason I didn't like them. And I think I have a responsibility as a blossom of art. To honor things about Gauguin's life and to work out philosophically how that determines the properties of his artwork, um. I wouldn't blame someone else like I, one of my friends actually, she didn't know, even up until recently, she didn't know of the about Gauguin's paintings of his that they were of his child brides, and that this knowledge massively affected her. Experience, and appreciation, perception even of those paintings, but I wouldn't, I was, I didn't blame her for not knowing that before, um, but she has a responsibility now to look at them differently.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Yeah, in the case of Goge, I mean, I guess it was not just the sexism and the racism, but also the colonial mentality that is promoted through his paintings, correct?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, and Gin is a tricky case because there are some uh scholars who have argued that he actually, his relationship with um, The locals in French Polynesia, in particular Tahiti and er Hivaa, er, a few of the places he lived, um, he had apparently a kind of knowledge about the local customs and particularly local names for people and deities, more he knew more about those groups than anthropologists did. So whilst we need to acknowledge that, that like his, his gaze wasn't entirely like. Um, DISMISSIVE of this group. I think he genuinely had a fascination, and, er, with, with these, um, with, with French Polynesian people. However, Despite that, we also need to, um, acknowledge the fact that when he went over there in his 40s, when he abandoned his wife and children, he was escaping France and what he saw is actually relevant to what you're saying before about depraved. He saw European culture is degenerate, and he saw French Polynesia as Eden, untouched by kind of toxic, uh, European civilization. But he also saw the people there as so-called savages. In his own diaries, he wrote of, um, Polynesian girls in particular as animal-like. Um, HE described them as snake-like and er deep down desiring rape as well. So, absolutely, he went over there, he took advantage of local, um, Customs to do with child marriage, which is peculiar because yes, it was kind of normal in the 19th century in French French Polynesia to sell girls into into marriage. And people like, well, it was OK. It's like, well, no, just because that particular civilization or or society thinks that that's OK, doesn't mean it is. The fact that he took advantage of that. And yet, the fact that that was still permitted, but other parts of their culture had been banned because French colonial rule by this point had overtaken Polynesia, uh, their customs and own kind of, uh, culture had been suppressed by that point. So when he actually got there, he was just Gauguin was disappointed because he wasn't that far away from, you know, Christian France at this point. So, he definitely took advantage of his, you know, colonial power as he had there. Yeah.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: So let's talk a little bit about depictions of sex and nudity, because I mean again for conservative people it's very common that they would label any depictions or almost any depictions of sex and. That has obscene. They use this word very often. So is it, do you think that it is right to judge artworks for the simple fact that they depict such scenes as obscene?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: No, absolutely not. And I think when we judge an artwork to be obscene, in part because of its sexual content, what we're at what's actually happening is that judgment derives normally from deep set prejudices or indeed, um, uh, an unfamiliarity with the artwork because it's showing sex in an unacceptable way. So if you compare, for instance, um. A lot of the sex or, um, erotic paintings of the Italian Renaissance, these paintings are glamorizing and eroticizing and in some cases sanitizing sexual violence. But because they were seen as like as part of the aristocratic or royal, um, they were often commissioned by royals. Um, FOR instance, you know, in Spain by King Philip the 2nd, um, They were treated as what the philosopher Anne Eaton notes in her paper on kind of the porn art divide as, um, honesto, like virtuous. So their sex is OK because it was for the upper classes, and that's OK. But if you, you know, once the invention of the printing press, particularly the German, not just the Chinese one, but the German one in particular, meant that a lot of kind of, um, saucy. Literature and prints as well, engravings started to reach the kind of lower rungs of society. That was seen as dishones though. So like, full of vice and problematic. So I think it's not, it's not just sex being depicted, it's who has ownership over it and who is being permitted to enjoy that. And this is also the case, um. When we have a clash of, uh, visions, in particular religious visions saturated with colonial mentality. So there's some amazing testimonies from medieval and early modern, uh, merchants who would, um, from Europe who would go to India and see some of the, um, Hindu and Jain, uh, temples that would, you know, show medieval, uh, Indian life, um, dancing, farming, um, food, things like that. But they're like few kind of sexually explicit carvings, for instance, the so-called Kama Sutra temples, which are actually called the Um, Kajuraho monuments, um, which are about 1000 years old. Only about 10% of those carvings show erotic sculpture. But because they were like very um explicit, but but really it wasn't the explicitness. I think that was the problem. It was the fact that it was this, um, non-Christian religion who saw sex differently to the kind of Christian mindset, um, of some of these travelers because sex, uh, and, you know, for instance, Hindu and Buddhist religion is not seen as a kind of um sinful thing to do. And it's not just it's to do with love and spirituality, it's not just to do with, um, procreation. So, And again it's about who has ownership over that sex as well, and this this this sort of um. Prejudice and double standards reaches all the way to like the 2020, um. Controversy around Cardi B and Megan Thee Stallion's rap song, um, which I'll let your viewers look at what, what that stands for, but, um, you know, it's a bombastic ode to female pleasure, um, but there was huge outcry against it because it, uh, it was women of color, expressing their sexual agency. And the fact that it's women of color in particular doing this, who have for centuries been hypersexualized, um, and dehumanized via a sexual lens, um, to see women of color taking back their sexual agency, that's what's problematic. Um. And a lot of the right wing commentators that denounced it, um, as pure depravity. One person said a woman, a woman, I think said that as well. Um, OTHER commentators said it was just extremely vulgar step back in feminist progress. But at the time, the, uh, President Donald Trump was normalizing sexual assault. So sex is OK. And if it's in the locker room, discussed in the men's locker room, but it's not OK if a woman of color celebrates sex. So it's not about sex per se, it's about who has power over it.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Yeah, there, there's definitely lots of hypocrisy going around, and I mean, since we're talking about sex and nudity, I guess that, that makes a great segue into the, the topic of pornography because I've been having these conversations on the show about that in the sense that, OK, so sometimes people Uh, condemn, there are even feminists who condemn pornography, but something that they might not even realize, or, I mean, some of them perhaps don't even care about it, but they might not even realize the fact that by condemning just Pornography in general, I mean, at the same time they might be condemning queer art, LGBTQ plus art, and artistic expressions, and even when it comes to the, for example, the sex workers themselves, if we're talking about porn actresses because they at least, I mean we could argue they also produce. Artworks, I mean, they might be condemning them to poorer working conditions and also for women more generally, women in general, they might be even, even if unintentionally aligning themselves with a patriarchal stance in the sense that they are more broadly condemning female sexuality and female. Sexual pleasure. I mean, there there are, there are several layers to this. I mean, I would like to hear your thoughts about that.

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, absolutely, you capture the kind of complexities really well and I think I, I'm definitely sympathetic to, uh, the concern around some genres of pornography, which we've got to think about a lot of the time when the so-called porn wars erupted in the. 70s and 80s. This sort of came out of, um, particularly in the West, a general concern about what Mary Whitehouse, the campaigner called the overly like permissive society. And Mary Whitehouse in the 60s was concerned that obscene, what she would call kind of obscene videos, but she was mainly railing against pornography, was corrupting minds and in particular young minds. Now she, Herself was homophobic, and she should be denounced for that, but some of her lobbying did result in something good like she criminalized, um, she, her lobbying resulted in legislation that criminalized child pornography. And that's a good thing, and I think what she was correct about, and this is sort of the side of the porn wars that I am on, people like Andrea Da Walkin, Catherine McKinnon writing that pornography causes, or as my, um, mentor Ray Langton has sort of popularized the view that it constitutes as well as causes harm towards women. And I think when said about particular genres of pornography, um, those that eroticize, so in in egalitarian pornography, so those those pornographic works that eroticize, um, patriarchal kind of conditioning of what sex should look like. That eroticizes non-consent, that expresses and peddles rape myths, that um sort of creates certain scripts of what particularly girls and women should look like. So the kind of body types and the norms and what good sex looks like. This is all bad. Um, AND this is mainly because these were the dominant genres of porn at the time in the 70s and 80s. A lot of porn that was kind of mainstream was doing this, and I think that's, we got to be conscious that that's I think what the porn wars were. And Railing against and loads of studies show that, That that type of pornography and the extreme types of it as well, including snuff films, um, do unfortunately, er distort and perhaps deform boys and girls' expectations of what good sex is, what the, you know, dismissing consent, um, what what a sexually attractive body should look like. And the studies are there and the and the philosophers you write about this, like for instance again Anne Eaton, when someone says this porn causes harm towards women, it's not like a simple sort of billiard ball, like one, like simple picture of causation, like one ball hits the other. It's not that a boy or a man watching porn is just gonna go out and do something terrible to a woman. Anne Eaton compares it to smoking, which I think is a really powerful model, so smoking just one cigarette is not going to give you cancer, but smoking cigarettes habitually over a long period of time will at least increase your risk of cancer. And so a similar kind of disease based. Uh, MODEL can be used for consuming this particular genre of pornography, and that is what the studies are showing, sadly. Um, PHILOSOPHERS of language in the last sort of 50 or so years have really started looking at, um, inspired by JL Austen in the 1950s, the Oxford philosopher, writing about how speech actually constitutes harm. And I think a lot of artworks are doing the same thing, they're performing. What these philosophers have called elocutionary acts. So these are the actions that are done in saying something. So it's not really about, I'm saying something and then something happens later later on in the world at a later time, that's of interest here. Like for instance, if you really just really liked Hitler's paintings, because they looked pretty, and a lot of people do think they look pretty, right, they might be a bit crap, but they, you know, they look pretty and peaceful and pleasant to look at. That I'd be like, yeah, OK, but you really ought to know. That actually this is a this is a depraved kind of immoral vision that he's giving us. It's actually very insidious, um. So there, you know, we might, I think we, we're gonna be able to criticize other people for making their artistic choices, what they choose to fill their lives with, um. And it's whether or not they're engaging with the artwork as a whole or if they're just taking it surface level, like oh I just I just really like the sound of Michael Jackson's music, so I'm just gonna carry on listening, it makes me wanna dance, so, but I think they're, you know, Well that's a tricky case anyway, but with say Hitler's paintings, you're not engaging with the artwork fully, and as long as you know that, then you know, I'm not going to stand behind you and police saying you're doing it wrong, kind of thing.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: Yeah, yeah, I, I mean for me personally, another example is Dentist Divine Comedy. I mean, it's just, for example, there's um one of the. Circles, circles of hell, there's arguably homosexuals there and there's also the circle of people who committed suicide, and I mean, of course I don't think that being homosexual is immoral at all, and I also don't condemn people who commit suicide, but I'm also like. But damn,

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: the poem is so good. Yeah, yeah, and I think this is, this is what philosophers about. I think this is why it's been tricky to bring this debate to the general public. People want very kind of like black and white, sort of almost sensationalist answers, like pro or anti. It's like actually no, artworks, because they're by their nature incredibly rich objects, they're gonna have multiple properties about them. You know, your example there, it's gonna have value. Is an artwork, undeniable value at the same time as having immoral properties, and it's a case, sometimes it's a case of weighing up those properties, like it's immoral immorality is just too much, and it ought not just that it does, but it ought to make us, um, either move away from it or not wish to consume it or indeed condemn it and things like that. Um, BUT you can see even the Marquis of Sars literature has value. But that's not, that's not just to say that is not to justify. Or sanitize the deep immorality within his works.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: So yeah, so when it comes to how we should deal with immoral artists or works of art, I have just two more questions I'd like to ask you. So do you think that censorship can be a solution?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Um, I think censorship, first of all, we need to get clear on what we mean by censorship. So it can mean anything from like suppression of an artwork, like stopping it from being either performed or shown an exhibition or indeed distributed, um. In any kind of form, but it can also mean outright destruction. So in that sense, if an artwork's already been made, and it's incredibly immoral, say it's oppressive or it incites violence or something. I don't think we should censor it in terms of destroying it. Um, AND in part that's because I think even depraved heritage, if you want to call it that, needs to be preserved for lots of reasons. Like I don't think we should be, especially in this day and age, we shouldn't be burning. Adolf Hitler's paintings. And there was on a Jimmy Carr show, a few years ago, an audience voted to destroy various works of art made by moral artists. They had an original watercolor by Adolf Hitler of a church, um, and the audience voted to destroy it, and he lowered it into like a row of chainsaws. I mean, the show was kind of problematic for lots of ways, kind of making a spectacle out of like iconoclasm, which was interesting in itself, but I don't think even Hitler's paintings should be destroyed. I don't think Gauguin's paintings should be destroyed or hidden in vaults, um. Because in part, even artworks that have been incredibly harmful, we need to preserve them, to learn from them, and they can act as a warning for where we're going again, especially with like the constant rise of the far right. A lot of these artworks like anti-Semitic propaganda, they need to be curated and handled sensitively. But to completely destroy them, there's a risk there of dismissing the role that art can play. In, say, genocide and social progress and liberation, um, so I think in that sense, just pure destruction, pure iconoclasm is not the right answer. And we also need to be even cautious about censorship in terms of suppression. Because in the past, the art that's often been censored, um, in that way is the kind of art we were talking about before, art that isn't actually immoral or a danger to society. Um, IT simply violates our social mores, our conventional norms. Um, A lot of queer art, for instance, has been censored across the world, er. You know, 11 example would be, um, in 1928, Radcliffe Hall's The Well of Loneliness novel was banned by UK courts. It wasn't even sexually explicit, I don't think. It's just because it was about a lesbian couple during the First World War. Um, BUT we also have cases like Megumi Gashi in Japan, um, who in 2014 was thrown in jail because she made an artwork in the shape of her own vulva. Um. And, you know, the vulva in Japan is still very, very taboo, whereas penises are perfectly acceptable, um, in society. And so a lot of the art that we wish to censor or suppress or indeed, um, prosecute an artist for making it. It's Just betraying deeper prejudices and biases against that person, and then also we have to be very worried about, Censoring art that's simply voicing, um, an opinion or a view that goes against a particular authoritarian regime, um. Uh, THE Nazi book burnings are a case in point of sort of censorship in the extreme form. They were just silencing dissent from anything that anything was taken to be, um, against the Nazi regime was destroyed. Um, AND this of course can be incredibly dangerous to silencing those important voices. So I think censorship in general we have to be wary of, but I do think in terms of incitement, We are right, it's, you know, if there is clear incitement there, it's, it's correct, it's correct to prosecute those artists. I think it's correct that. Julius Stryker was um charged with incitement to genocide in the Nuremberg trials through the art that he was publishing. Um, SO in a legal way, there's still things that can be done there that might be deemed a kind of censorship, um, or indeed prevention from making the art in the first place. If it's harming animals or humans in the process, then yes, we should stop that.

speaker: Ricardo Lopes
words: OK, so one final question. I mean, leaving just aside for a second, the legality of it, I mean, about whether legally we should prohibit certain kinds of artistic expression or certain kinds of behaviors by artists and all of that. I mean, just normatively, I mean you. You're a philosopher of art. You're an artist and a consumer of art as I am. So do you think that in terms of norms, in terms of how we should normatively approach art and artists, do you think that we should really approach art as if we think or if we assume that should be absolute freedom of expression in art. I mean, because personally I think that sometimes there's a tendency, at least among certain art appreciators, let's say, of putting artists on a pedestal and wherever they explore in their artwork. OR sometimes even whatever kind of behavior they have just because they have a certain social status that you shouldn't question it. Oh, it's he's an artist. I mean, he's doing his thing. I mean, don't bother him. And then we have, we have cases like Gauguin and Picasso and We have, for example, what they did to the actress in The Last Tango in Paris and all of that. So, and, and sometimes people just dismiss it as, 000 it's just art and they're just artists. I mean, don't you think that perhaps sometimes we should be a little bit less permissive of certain kinds of artistic expression?

speaker: Daisy Dixon
words: Yeah, definitely. So I, in in the book and also just generally, I've always been very anti this kind of the genius myth. Like, yes, these artists, you know, are are particular canons, particularly the Western canon, are dominated by, uh, they do have masterpieces. Yes, these artists were incredibly talented. Um. But this idea that they're somehow otherworldly, like they're particularly special human beings that um, get a moral pass and don't, I, I've always been really against that, and I think it's it's it's tendency again to treat. Artworks as somehow sacred. Uh, AND you know, in many cultures, in many groups, artworks are sacred, and I'm obviously completely understand that. But sacred in a way that means they're not connected to the local, the community that consumes it. That's what I don't agree with. Um, THIS idea that art is somehow separate from the world, and that it's this sort of entirely insulated. Um, PRACTICE where anything, anything goes, and I think in terms of, When an artist has done wrong either in the view that they're expressing in the artwork or indeed the very process of creating it was unethical, I just think we should treat them as we would, A human doing just a normal non-artistic action, I think. This is where I guess, you know, complicated art is special in that. They're going back to the kind of first question you asked me, like artworks are these peculiar kind of objects that are rich in meaning. And I think this is the this is the problem going back to this question of censorship. A lot of the time it's really hard, especially with incitement, it's hard to find the artists can kind of easily wiggle their way out of it because it's not straightforward communication a lot of the time. It is a kind of invitation to often a fictional world that can have all sorts of meanings and values that people can glean from it, um. But I think there are still gonna be clear cases where the way it's been made and the and the view it expresses is harmful to society, um, and absolutely we shouldn't be overly permissive just because it's art. And indeed, you know, this is sort of taking in a slightly outdated view about so-called high art. These artworks are no longer locked away in private collections in aristocratic homes. In the dawn of the museum in the 19th century, these artworks were made free in the West to many parts of the general public. Um, THE printing press meant that all sorts of novels and pictures were making their way through across the world. And, Yeah, because of that, it just seems like art is an intimate part of our world. It affects how we treat and see each other. Anything from beautiful paintings to cartoons and um. You know, general artifacts that are sort of on the brink, you know, sort of artworks, but also sort of not, these all these things really shape our moral compass in a way that we have to take seriously what they're doing. But I think the ethics of the ethics of censorship depends on the ethics of the artwork, um. Obviously, clear incitement to genocide shouldn't be permitted, um. But in other cases, if an art if an artwork is expressing a problematic perspective about a person, that's why in the book I suggest perhaps a better solution is a so-called counter speech, um, to kind of disarm and destroy that work, that artwork without physically destroying it. Um, AND that that might be a way to get around this problem of censorship, um. Which has its own problems.
